---
title: "About"
---

I am a psychology lecturer and researcher whose work focuses on eyewitness identification, memory, decision-making, and the application of computational approaches to legal evidence.

My research centres on a broader programme aimed at developing scientifically grounded methods for estimating eyewitness reliability and translating those findings into evidence-based guidance, training, policy, and practice within the justice system.

A central goal of this work is to move beyond qualitative assessments of eyewitness evidence towards transparent, interpretable, and empirically calibrated estimates of reliability that can support more informed legal decision-making.

## Research Programme

My research programme integrates cognitive psychology, memory science, decision science, computational modelling, metacognition, and legal decision-making research.

Current areas of research include:

- Witnessing conditions and identification accuracy
- Confidence and metacognition
- Evidence accumulation and decision processes
- Probabilistic modelling and reliability estimation
- Juror decision-making and evidence evaluation
- Ecological validity and virtual reality

Together, these strands contribute to a translational research programme progressing from the study of eyewitness memory and decision-making towards the development of practical methods for estimating and communicating eyewitness reliability.

## Research Philosophy

My approach combines basic psychological science with applied legal questions.

I am particularly interested in research that not only advances theoretical understanding of cognition and decision-making, but also has the potential to improve professional practice, public understanding, and legal decision-making.

This includes developing models and frameworks that are transparent, interpretable, empirically grounded, and capable of supporting evidence evaluation in real-world settings.

## Teaching

Alongside research, I teach undergraduate and postgraduate psychology, with particular interests in cognitive psychology and quantitative research methods. I have regularly taught all BPS core curriculum areas, as well as specialist modules including forensic psychology and cyberpsychology.

My teaching philosophy emphasises engagement, accessibility, confidence-building, and active discussion. I aim to create learning environments that are intellectually challenging while remaining supportive and inclusive for students from diverse backgrounds.

I receive consistently strong student feedback and have frequently achieved the highest average student performance across the modules I teach when compared with parallel teaching teams.

## Future Directions

Future work will extend the programme through:

- Predictive reliability estimation
- Individual differences in eyewitness performance
- Immersive virtual reality methodologies
- Communication of probabilistic evidence
- Translation of research into professional guidance, policy, and decision-support tools

The long-term ambition is to develop scientifically grounded and transparent methods for estimating eyewitness reliability and to contribute to more informed evaluation of eyewitness evidence within the justice system.

## Contact

For research collaborations, funding opportunities, student supervision, or general enquiries, please get in touch.

sfarmer@arden.ac.uk
